title.en: Structured Outputs & Function Calling with OpenAI

description.en: Turn free-text generation into reliable machine-readable data: OpenAI's JSON modes, schema-enforced Structured Outputs, function calling — and the validation discipline production systems still need.

story_variants.en:
## The Integration Problem

The hardest part of putting an LLM inside a business system was never the intelligence — it was the *interface*. Software downstream of the model wants fields, types and enums; models naturally produce prose. Ask for JSON politely and you get JSON... usually... wrapped in markdown fences, or with a chatty preamble, or with a field renamed on Tuesdays. At demo scale you laugh; at a million calls a month, a 2% malformed-output rate is twenty thousand broken records.

OpenAI attacked this problem in stages, and the progression is worth knowing because you will meet code written at every stage.

## From Prompt-and-Pray to Schema Enforcement

- **Stage 1: prompting.** "Respond ONLY with valid JSON." Works most of the time; "most" is the problem.
- **Stage 2: JSON mode.** The API constrains output to be syntactically valid JSON. Parseable, yes — but nothing guarantees *your* fields or types appear.
- **Stage 3: Structured Outputs.** You attach a **JSON Schema** to the request, and the platform constrains token generation so the output *conforms to the schema* — required fields present, types respected, enums enforced. This works via constrained decoding: at each step, tokens that would violate the schema are masked out.

Structured Outputs was a genuine reliability breakthrough, and competitors have converged on similar capabilities. But schema conformance is not correctness: the model can emit a perfectly valid record whose *values* are wrong — a confident, well-typed hallucination. Schema guarantees shape; only evals and downstream checks guard truth.

> **Callout:** Not every schema feature is supported in constrained mode (deeply recursive structures, certain formats and unions have limits), and the first call with a new schema pays a compilation overhead. Test your actual schema, not a toy.

## Function Calling: Structured Outputs Pointed at Actions

**Function calling** (tool calling) is the same machinery aimed at *doing* instead of *reporting*: you declare functions with JSON-Schema parameters; the model responds with a tool call — name plus arguments — your code executes it and returns the result; the model continues. Conceptually identical to Claude's tool_use loop from the previous module, differing in wire format: OpenAI returns `tool_calls` with **arguments as a JSON string you must parse**, and results go back as tool-role messages keyed by call ID. The Responses API additionally offers **built-in tools** — web search, file search, code execution — that run server-side with no dispatch code of yours.

| Mechanism | Guarantees | Still your job |
|---|---|---|
| Prompted JSON | Nothing | Everything |
| JSON mode | Syntactic validity | Fields, types, values |
| Structured Outputs | Schema conformance | Value correctness, business rules |
| Function calling | Schema-shaped arguments | Validation, execution, authorization |

## Why Validation Refuses to Die

A tempting conclusion: "the platform enforces schemas now, so I can delete my validation layer." Resist it. Production systems keep validating because:

- **Business rules exceed schemas.** `priority` being an integer is schema; `priority` being 1-5 *and consistent with the SLA field* is business logic.
- **Defense in depth.** You will switch models, providers or API modes someday; the validator is what stays constant.
- **Function arguments feed real systems.** An integer that is validly-typed but references someone else's account ID passes the schema and fails security. Authorization checks belong *after* parsing, *before* execution.
- **Legacy and multi-provider paths** may not use constrained decoding at all.

The standard Python practice is a Pydantic model (or dataclass plus checks) as the single source of truth: generate the JSON Schema *from* it for the API call, then parse responses back *through* it. One definition, both directions.

> **Adoption pitfall:** Schema design is prompt engineering in disguise. Field *names and descriptions* are read by the model — `"dt"` yields worse extractions than `"delivery_date: ISO 8601 date the customer requested"`. Teams that treat schemas as mere plumbing leave accuracy on the table.

## A Worked Pattern: Ticket Extraction

The canonical enterprise example: turn a rambling support email into `{name: str, priority: int}`. The model handles language; the schema guarantees shape; and a thin validator enforces the rest — required fields present, types correct. That validator is deliberately boring code: loop over required fields, check membership, check `isinstance`, collect human-readable errors. Boring is the point — it runs on every record, never gets tired, and its error strings become your data-quality dashboard.

In the challenge you will write exactly that validator: `validate(record, schema)` returning a list of error strings, applied to three records of varying health, with a deterministic printed report. It is the unglamorous half of Structured Outputs — and the half that pages you at 3 a.m. if you skip it.

One closing note of vendor balance: OpenAI's structured-output developer experience is widely considered excellent, but the concepts here are portable — Claude enforces JSON via tool schemas, Gemini has native structured output with response schemas. Learn the pattern once, apply it on all three.

description.en: The reliability ladder from prose to schema-conformant data — with validation as the gate before anything touches real systems.

left.label: Schema conformance means the data is correct — delete the validators

right.label: Schema guarantees shape; validators and evals still guard values, business rules and authorization

description.en: A well-typed hallucination passes every schema check. Defense in depth is not optional.

explanation.en: Walk the schema's required fields — the contract downstream systems depend on.
Missing fields are reported by name; error strings double as data-quality metrics.
isinstance enforces the type contract — a string 'high' is not an integer priority.
Return ALL errors, not just the first: batch pipelines need the full picture per record.

description.en: A validation report over three extracted records — the boring loop that keeps twenty thousand broken records out of your CRM.
The production extraction pipeline: schema out, data in, validation in between.

instruction.en: Write the validation layer that backs every structured-output pipeline. Complete validate(record, schema) where schema = {'required': [...], 'types': {field: type}}: for each required field IN ORDER, append 'missing <field>' if the field is absent, else append 'wrong type <field>' if isinstance fails; return the list of errors (empty list means valid). Then, for the three records in RECORDS in order (named record_1, record_2, record_3), print EXACTLY four lines: '<record_name>: valid' when there are no errors or '<record_name>: <errors joined by "; ">' otherwise, followed by 'valid_count=' plus the number of valid records. At production volume, this loop is what stands between model output and your CRM.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
SCHEMA = {
    "required": ["name", "priority"],
    "types": {"name": str, "priority": int},
}

RECORDS = [
    ("record_1", {"name": "reset password", "priority": 2}),
    ("record_2", {"name": "billing question"}),
    ("record_3", {"name": "api outage", "priority": "high"}),
]

def validate(record, schema):
    errors = []
    # TODO: for each field in schema["required"] (in order):
    #   - if field not in record: append "missing <field>"
    #   - elif wrong type: append "wrong type <field>"
    return errors

# TODO: for each (name, record) in RECORDS print "<name>: valid"
#       or "<name>: <errors joined by '; '>"
# TODO: print "valid_count=<number of records with no errors>"
practice_challenges.expected_output:
record_1: valid
record_2: missing priority
record_3: wrong type priority
valid_count=1

en: Check membership first (field not in record), and only check isinstance in the elif — a missing field has no type.
Use isinstance(record[field], schema["types"][field]) for the type check.
Track a counter: if not errors: valid += 1 and print "valid"; else print "; ".join(errors) after the name.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert validate({"name": "x", "priority": 1}, SCHEMA) == []
assert validate({"name": "x"}, SCHEMA) == ["missing priority"]
assert validate({"name": "x", "priority": "high"}, SCHEMA) == ["wrong type priority"]
assert validate({}, SCHEMA) == ["missing name", "missing priority"]
assert validate({"name": 7, "priority": 1}, SCHEMA) == ["wrong type name"]

q.en: What does OpenAI's JSON mode guarantee that plain prompting does not?

en: That your required fields are present
That the output is syntactically valid JSON
That the response fits in one token

q.en: How do Structured Outputs enforce schema conformance?

en: By retrying until the output validates
By post-processing with a repair model
By constrained decoding — masking tokens that would violate the attached JSON Schema during generation

q.en: Why do production systems keep a validation layer even with schema-enforced outputs?

en: Because schema conformance guarantees shape, not value correctness, business rules or authorization

q.en: In OpenAI function calling, what does the API return when the model decides to use a tool?

en: The executed result of the function
A tool call with the function name and arguments (as a JSON string) for YOUR code to parse, validate and execute
